items.title: Had an interaction with Promoters of Milworks on some social media concerns, this is what was clarified: 1. Electricity costs are not increasing to sa...
items.description_html:
Had an interaction with Promoters of Milworks on some social media concerns, this is what was clarified:

1. Electricity costs are not increasing to sales as more growth came from assemblies and sub-assemblies which do not require power. Also no new machines were added in last 8 mths

2. Company does work with Siemens, Alstom, WABTEC hence there is no direct billing to Indian Railways

3. Due to logistics and geopolitical situation, billing and delivery for a large Israeli orders is delayed hence increase in receivables

4. Expect payments from Quick Pay, Israeli Customers in next few weeks easing working capital ahead.

5. Lots of exciting opportunities in Defence, Aerospace Industry expected.

items.title: One thing that I keep working on and have seen some results is not to get in too early in a theme /company especially in the Pharma/Chemicals segment ...
items.description_html:
One thing that I keep working on and have seen some results is not to get in too early in a theme /company especially in the Pharma/Chemicals segment but also in most sectors

And why this is important comes from inflection point or rate of change , good companies with a strong thesis either have to be bought too cheap or bought higher but with confidence of the 12 quarter runway intact

The middle ground where there is no clarity is the places where I avoid , I have seen many folks get into good companies at no time based return strategy , remember time is a variable in returns , a thesis and entry point and business inflection point time has to align , otherwise mistakes can be expensive !

And I am not a charity investor , that I buy and hope that things turn around and even if they do my payoff has to be time adjusted , good companies 3 years no return then one month it is up 30% what is the use of these returns

Focus on time adjusted returns

items.description_html:
RT CSEINDIA
Two cloudburst occurred minutes apart in the Anantnag district of Kashmir, causing lasting damage.

Flash floods in Kashmir's Anantnag district washed away roads, damaged homes, and hotels, forcing evacuations. They also caused extensive damage to agricultural fields and apple orchards, which serve as the primary livelihood for local farmers. As extreme rainfall events become more frequent, understanding why they happen is more important than ever.

items.title: Across most of my investment interactions, a question almost always comes up - which sector looks good to you? I try to talk my investment framework (...
items.description_html:
Across most of my investment interactions, a question almost always comes up - which sector looks good to you?

I try to talk my investment framework (which is sector agnostic - more horizontal than vertical), and the person abruptly asks - "Banks look good, right?" (Almost wanting me to say yes).

I find no right or wrong answer to this question. Well, they don't look bad to me - credit growth is improving, FCNR likely to help (atleast sentimentally).

But the space doesnt excite me naturally to say thumpingly - "Yes, buy Banks." Valuations have sobered over the years, but for real reasons. I admire this series of posts by Amit below and what exactly has been pressuring the valuation for Banks, even though profits grew. Any serious investor should read them.


Amit Kumar Gupta: Fee Income Is Getting Squeezed by the Regulator

Banks have historically supplemented their interest income with a wide range of fees — distribution of mutual funds and insurance, processing fees, forex income, card-related charges, and the like. Many of these are now under

items.title: Growth is returning in a big way for domestic pharma Pharma: Domestic sales – Sharp growth pickup in Q1; volume driven The Apr-June IPM data points t...
items.description_html:
Growth is returning in a big way for domestic pharma

Pharma: Domestic sales – Sharp growth pickup in Q1; volume driven

The Apr-June IPM data points to a broad-based acceleration in the domestic formulations market, driven by continued outperformance in chronic therapies, improving volume contribution and healthy growth across companies.

◦ Strongest monthly growth in last several quarters at 16% YoY for IPM

◦ The strong June performance also lifted IPM Q1FY27 growth to ~14% YoY (vs the high-single to low-double-digit trend seen over the past several quarters), marking a clear inflection in the domestic formulations market.

◦ Chronic therapies continue to lead outperformance
· Growth remained chronic led, with chronic therapies growing at 20% YoY vs 13% for acute

◦ Healthy double-digit growth across companies in June/Q1FY27

Q1FY27 - with Sun (+15%), Cipla (+15%), Mankind (+13%), Torrent (+16%), Lupin (+17%), Dr. Reddy's (+15%), Alkem (+14%), Glenmark (+18%) and IPCA (+12%) reporting healthy growth. Among MNCs, Abbott (+14%), GSK (+11%), Pfizer (+12%), Sanofi (+10%), PGH (+15%) and AstraZeneca (+47%) continued to post strong growth

items.title: RT Sridhar Vembu: Last week, I was fortunate to visit the workshop (the Tamil word pattarai பட்டறை is so appropriate) of the leading motor desig...
items.description_html:
RT Sridhar Vembu
Last week, I was fortunate to visit the workshop (the Tamil word pattarai பட்டறை is so appropriate) of the leading motor designer in the world Morihiro Saito-san.

Saito-san is 80+ and still designing advanced motors, some of the most advanced in the world. His modest looking workshop is right next door to a company that builds out those motors in prototype quantities and he works closely with them to get them built. I visited as an industrial "pilgrim", with as much devotion as I go to the Thoranamalai Murugan Temple at home. When I saw him at work, I was moved to touch his feet.

Saito-san, the God of motors, is how Japan is able to retain its global prominence in so many foundational industries.

We need to learn from that spirit. We need to revive our "pattarai" culture of building things - every village in India needs such workshops!

My industrial pilgrimage to Japan has given me new vigor 🙏

WPI inflation hardens to a series high of 9.9% in June 2026 from 9.7% in May 2026, amid a higher-than-expected fuel and power print.
Food inflation surges to 18-month high of 6.1% in June 2026, pushing up the headline print by as much as 43 bps relative to May 2026 — partly offset by the non-food components of the WPI. Core WPI expectedly eases to 7.5% in June 2026 from 7.7% in previous month, amid some sequential softening in commodity prices.

items.title: China’s trade data for June were pretty surprising. Exports were $412.4 billion, up 27% year on year, while imports were $286.8, up 36%. Xinhua repor...
items.description_html:
China’s trade data for June were pretty surprising. Exports were $412.4 billion, up 27% year on year, while imports were $286.8, up 36%.

Xinhua reports that the fact that growth in imports exceeded growth in exports helped "contribute to balanced trade development", but its hard to see the resulting balance in the trade numbers. China's trade surplus in June was $125.6, the second highest monthly surplus ever recorded by any country, beaten only by the $138.6 billion recorded by China in January, 2025 (mainly because the timing of that year's Spring Festival shifted most of the trade scheduled for the first two months of the year into January).

Still, the six-month trade surplus in 2026 is down a little from 2025 (by just over 1%) even as exports are up 18% and imports up 26%. With so much uncertainty, it is hard to make confident predictions for the rest of the year, but China's trade surplus in 2026 seems easily on track to break $1 trillion again.

June's surge in imports probably had more to do with rising import prices (especially semiconductors) than with rising domestic demand, which has remained very weak so far this year. We'll see however if strong import growth can be sustained over the next few months. If so, that will be good news for the rest of the world.
https://sc.mp/ucj94?utm_source=twitter&utm_campaign=3360451&utm_medium=share_widget

items.description_html:
Caixin: "China’s cabinet has approved a policy plan to expand domestic consumption, setting a goal of lifting annual retail sales of consumer goods to about 60 trillion yuan by 2030."

With China’s total retail sales of consumer goods having reached 50.12 trillion yuan in 2025, according to the NBS, to achieve this requires that it grow annually on average over the next five years by 3.7%. In 2025 it grew by 3.7% year on year, and by 1.2% in the first five months of 2026. Retail sales of services in the first five months grew much faster, by 5.4%.
https://www.caixinglobal.com/2026-07-14/china-targets-60-trillion-yuan-in-retail-sales-by-2030-102463910.html

items.description_html:
HCL announced data centre plans

-To make an investment of ₹3,500 crore; to float a subsidiary and to build its own AI data centres in India with the potential to scale to 50MW of capacity

-Management says the convergence of AI-led demand, supply constraints, and push for digital sovereignty presented a compelling opportunity for the company to emerge as a full-stack AI technology solutions provider

-The company is looking to monetize the entire solution, not just space and power, by focusing on a full-stack AI solution with high-margin services, rather than just renting out capacity.

-Data center investment will be funded through equity and debt. Not funding it at the expense of the company’s dividend policy

-This follows their USD150m investment in Sarvam, India's sovereign LLM play

TCS also spoke about Sovereign AI

Good to see IT companies take steps towards new age sectors!

items.title: Risk appetite is reaching historic levels: The 10-day moving average of S&P 500 options volume is up to a record ~5.5 million contracts. Since 2022, t...
items.description_html:
Risk appetite is reaching historic levels:

The 10-day moving average of S&P 500 options volume is up to a record ~5.5 million contracts.

Since 2022, this figure has more than TRIPLED.

Over the same period, Euro Stoxx 50 options volume has remained roughly unchanged, near ~1 million contracts.

As a result, US options volumes are now more than 5 times larger than that of Europe.

By comparison, options activity in these two markets moved in tandem before 2022.

The US options market boom is unprecedented.

items.title: RT وزارة الدفاع |MOD UAE: تعلن وزارة الدفاع عن تعرض الناقلتين الوطنيتين (ممباسا) و (البا...
items.description_html:
RT وزارة الدفاع |MOD UAE
تعلن وزارة الدفاع عن تعرض الناقلتين الوطنيتين (ممباسا) و (الباهية) للاستهداف بصاروخين جوالين إيرانيين في الممر الجنوبي لمضيق هرمز بالمياه الإقليمية العمانية.

وقد أسفر الاستهداف عن مقتل أحد أفراد طاقم الناقلة (ممباسا) من الجنسية الهندية، وإصابة 8 من بينهم 4 إصابات بليغة. (6 من الجنسية الهندية، و2 من الجنسية الأوكرانية).

كما أسفر الاستهداف عن أضرار مادية بالناقلتين نتيجة نشوب الحريق بهما، وقد تم السيطرة على الحريق في الناقلتين.

وأدانت وزارة الدفاع هذا الهجوم السافر الذي يمثل انتهاكاً خطيراً، وخرقاً واضحاً للقانون الدولي ويهدد أمن واستقرار المنطقة، مشددة على أن الدولة تحتفظ بحقها الكامل في الرد على هذا التصعيد واتخاذ جميع الإجراءات اللازمة لحماية أراضيها وشعبها والمقيمين فيها، بما يضمن صون سيادتها وأمنها واستقرارها ويحمي مصالحها ومقدراتها الوطنية.

وأعربت الوزارة أنها على أهبة الاستعداد والجاهزية للتعامل مع أية تهديدات، وأنها تتخذ كافة الإجراءات اللازمة للتصدي بحزم لكل ما يستهدف زعزعة أمن الدولة واستقرارها.

وتهيب الوزارة بالجمهور الكريم استقاء المعلومات من المصادر الرسمية في الدولة، وتجنب تداول الشائعات أو المعلومات غير الموثوقة.

#وزارة_الدفاع
#وزارة_الدفاع_الإماراتية
#MOD
#UAEMinistryOfDefence

Crypto funds are showing their first signs of a recovery:

Crypto ETFs attracted +$281.8 million in inflows last week, the first weekly inflow since the 2nd week of May.

Bitcoin funds posted +$197.4 million in inflows, while Ethereum funds attracted +$84.4 million.

This also marks the end of an 8-week streak of outflows, totaling more than -$7 billion.

As a result, trailing 12-month inflows are down to +$1 billion, from +$10 billion in late April.

By comparison, inflows peaked at +$12 billion in October 2025.

Buyers are beginning to return to the crypto market.

items.title: Bajaj Consumer Care Q1FY27 Earnings Call Update •Q1FY27 Revenue grew 28% YoY, while EBITDA margin expanded to 24.7% despite raw material inflation le...
items.description_html:
Bajaj Consumer Care Q1FY27 Earnings Call Update

•Q1FY27 Revenue grew 28% YoY, while EBITDA margin expanded to 24.7% despite raw material inflation led by operating leverage.

•Q2 margins to remain soft due to high-cost inventory; management expects qoq improvement from Q3 onwards.

•Future margin improvement will be driven by operating leverage only, as no further price increase is expected in the near term.

•Almond Drops Hair Oil (ADHO) posted low-teen volume growth, led by strong traction in sachets, smaller packs and across channels. Also, there was a positive movement across all consumer metrics.

•It has delivered a broad-based growth across channels like General Trade, Modern Trade, e-commerce. Rural has performed in line with urban growth. Also, international markets, such as Nepal and Bangladesh continued its double-digit growth.

•Growth portfolio (Coconut & Banjara's) remains a key focus, with distribution-led expansion. Banjara would be a second largest in its growth portfolio, where the plan is still under process which will be announced once finalised.

•Aarohan continues to aid distribution expansion, delivering 200–300 bps incremental growth as a one-time impact when rollout happens.

•Brand investments and promotional activities remain unchanged, with ad spends expected to remain in the range of 15–16% of sales.

•Management aspired to operate in Low to mid-twenties margin, as stated earlier. As business is dynamic, no yearly guidance is provided.

Stock is trading at P/E of 32.6x FY28E EPS - NBRR Note

“Thanks to the U.S. military and President Trump, oil is moving through the Strait of Hormuz. Yesterday, 8.5 million barrels of oil transited through the Strait, consistent with the recent average. items.description_html:
HCLT 1QFY27 (first-cut): Results marginally better on all key parameters

HCLT reported revenue decline of 0.5 % QoQ in CC vs our expectations of -1.1%. (Consensus: -1.1%)

*In YoY organic terms*, cc revenue was up 2.6 % versus Accenture (+1.5), TCS (+2.6%)

*Revenue guidance for FY27 intact* – Revenue +1.0% to +4.0% y/y CC vs inline with our expectations. Services Revenue growth expected to be 1.5% - 4.5% YoY in CC inline with our expectations.

*Margin guidance for FY27 intact* at 17.5-18.5%, in line with our expectations.

*Adjusted EBIT margins for 1Q at 17.5%* (ex-restructuring; reported 16.9%) versus our expectation of adjusted 16.9%. (Consensus: 16.8%)

*Deals wins slightly ahead with our expectations:* New Deal TCV was USD2.4bn vs our expectation of USD2.2bn and 4 quarter average of USD 2.3bn. (consensus: USD 2-2.5bn)

*Segment wise* ER&D Services (-3.7% in CC terms), and Software (+2.2 %), while IT Services revenues flattish QoQ.

*Vertical and geo performance:* Vertical – Financial (+2.2% QoQ in USD terms), Mfg (-0.5%), Healthcare (-2.4%), Tech (-3.7%), Telecom (-8.4%), Retail (+5.1%), Public Services (~flat)

*Geo* – Americas (-1.6% QoQ in USD terms), Europe (+0.8%), ROW (-5.4%), India (+12.6%)

*Other highlights for 1Q*
•Attrition (LTM) at 12.7%; up by 20bps QoQ.
•Client Metrics: USD100mn+ clients were flat QoQ, while USD50mn+ clients were flat QoQ.
•DSO improved by 2 days QoQ to 59 days.
HCL declared interim dividend of Rs. 12/- per equity share.
FCF/NI at 99% vs 121% last quarter.

We currently have a HOLD, with a TP of INR 1,200 (valuing at 15x FY28E earnings)

items.title: Here's how copper recycling value chain looks like - moves from sourcing the raw material through processing and finally finished product delivery 1. ...
items.description_html:
Here's how copper recycling value chain looks like - moves from sourcing the raw material through processing and finally finished product delivery

1. Scrap sourcing (input)
The process begins with globally sourced copper-bearing wire and cable scrap rather than clean domestic copper cathode. Driven by the global replacement of older telecom infrastructure with fibre-optics, this cheaper scrap is harder to process due to plastic and aluminum contaminants. Effectively pricing and selecting these quality-varying lots requires expertise built over decades of supplier relationships.

2. Segregation and pre-processing
Incoming scrap is sorted and stripped of materials like plastic, aluminium, and tin prior to melting. This stage generates significant byproduct volumes (such as plastic granules), which recyclers utilize as secondary value-add opportunities.

3. Melting and refining
Sorted scrap is melted and fire-refined across furnaces to achieve near-zero oxygen content, a critical requirement for specialized electrical and mechanical applications. High-end producers rely on continuous-casting technology originating from Finnish suppliers to manage this quality differentiation.

4. Casting rod and ingot
Refined copper is cast into low-margin, commodity-tier rods or ingots sold to traders, contractors, or cable manufacturers. Versatile producers operate this as a swing business, running it during favorable spreads or redirecting capacity to higher-margin products.

5. Drawing and rolling (semi-finished forms)
Cast rod is processed through dies into fine wire or rolled into strips. Requiring low-oxygen copper feed, this step differentiates the metal into feedstock for specialized applications like motor windings and transformer components.

6. Coating and fabrication (value-added stage)
This high-margin stage involves coating wire or strip (enamel, tin, or silver) and fabricating them into components like busbars and Continuously Transposed Conductors (CTC). Plating choices balance cost and reliability: tin offers cost-efficient corrosion resistance, while premium silver plating is deployed for high-conductivity, zero-downtime needs like AI data-centre infrastructure.

7. Testing and quality assurance
Critical products like CTC undergo intensive inline testing regimes because applications in data centres and power transformers have zero tolerance for field failure. A single defect can compromise the entire conductor.

8. Distribution to end markets
Finished products are supplied to OEMs in electrical, automotive, renewable energy, and data-centre sectors. Economics compress downstream: commodity rod/ingot margins sit at 1–3%, while multi-process, value-added products command 5–10%+, with data-centre busbars yielding nearly double the blended average.

Spanning from sourcing to fabrication, a recycler managing this full process captures value at every step compared to those stopping at basic commodities. This structural logic explains the focus on a high value-added product (VAP) mix.

items.title: For my Chinese friends, CITIC Press, one of China’s largest and most prestigious commercial publishers, has just reissued the Chinese translation of ...
items.description_html:
For my Chinese friends, CITIC Press, one of China’s largest and most prestigious commercial publishers, has just reissued the Chinese translation of Paul Volcker and Toyoo Gyohten's 1992 book, "Changing Fortunes", for which I was asked to write an introduction.

This account of global financial policy after the breakdown of Bretton Woods and during the Japanese economic miracle was written at the very beginning of Japan's difficult post-1991 adjustment by two of the key players during the period. For that reason, it should be read by anyone in China interested in understanding the Plaza Accord and how Japan adjusted from the deep domestic imbalances, the soaring debt, and the massive trade surpluses of the 1980s. This story has important implications for China, whose imbalances are similar to (and much greater than) those of Japan in the 1980s.

items.description_html:
RT CSEINDIA
There is clear evidence that India is getting hotter. The number of extreme heat days is projected to rise by nearly 50 per cent, with devastating consequences for people, livelihoods and the economy.

CSE- @down2earthindia analysis of internal displacement in India clearly shows that climate-related disasters will increasingly force people from their homes. These data points compel us to ask, “What do we need to do next?” and to act on the answer.

India currently experiences about 48 extremely hot days each year. Even if countries meet their Paris Agreement pledges, this could rise to 71 days under a 2.6°C warming scenario. Nine states are projected to see 50 to 138 more hot days by end of the century, and 14 states could see 30 to 49 more hot days.

Read more in the State of India's Environment in Figures 2026: https://csestore.cse.org.in/default/state-of-india-s-environment-2026-in-figures-print.html

items.title: Although I agree with much of Milanovic says about inequality, I disagree with him here. I think he is confusing "efficient" manufacturing with "compe...
items.description_html:
Although I agree with much of Milanovic says about inequality, I disagree with him here. I think he is confusing "efficient" manufacturing with "competitive" manufacturing.

China is not necessarily building things better and more cheaply than Germany or France, but it is certainly selling them far more cheaply, and the difference shows up both in the extremely low share households receive of what they produce and in the astonishing rise in China's debt-to-GDP ratio. This was the same strategy Japan followed in the 1980s, and not only was it unsustainable, but the high debt and low consumption share ultimately forced Japan into an extraordinarily difficult adjustment.

If Germany and France were willing to suppress wages (or, which is the same thing, to eliminate social transfers), or if they were willing to borrow comparable amounts to subsidize the competitiveness of their manufacturers, it is pretty obvious that French and German manufacturers would also be able to sell much more cheaply in global markets.

But while these policies would increase manufacturing competitiveness, they would not make manufacturing any more efficient. They would simply shift part of the economic costs of production onto the rest of the country.
https://michaelpettis858496.substack.com/p/mckinsey-confuses-efficiency-and


Branko Milanovic: Nobody can take seriously European complaints about China. China is now building things better and more cheaply than Germany or France. When France and Germany built things better and more cheaply than others, they ridiculed such complaints. Now they are losing and not liking

items.title: Very good piece by Joshua Busby on China's EV production. I only have one quibble. Busby says: "Xiaomi’s attempt to take on Apple and Tesla seems lik...
items.description_html:
Very good piece by Joshua Busby on China's EV production. I only have one quibble. Busby says: "Xiaomi’s attempt to take on Apple and Tesla seems like one of those only-in-China stories. China’s scale of manufacturing — and the vast supplier ecosystems this sustains — make China arguably the only country where a mobile phone maker can try to become an EV maker as well."

I would argue that the real reason a mobile phone maker in China can quickly pivot to becoming a major EV maker is because of near-unlimited financing at very accommodative terms. Anyone in China interested in expanding production capabilities in sectors deemed strategic by the government can raise enormous amounts of financing very easily, with little concern about eventually hitting hard budget constraints.

But China's ability to do this wasn't an only-in-China story. It was also the story of Japan in the 1970s and 1980s. Every time Tokyo deemed a manufacturing sector to be of strategic importance, Japanese manufacturers quickly dominated that sector globally.

For a while Japanese manufacturers in one sector after another seemed unassailable, but ultimately a country's debt capacity is always the constraint. No country can maintain global competitiveness forever if this competitiveness requires permanent increases in debt to fund the sources of its competitiveness (i.e., direct and indirect subsidies). The cost to Japan was a surge in overall debt that ultimate caused the whole strategy to reverse after 1990-91 in the form of an extremely difficult adjustment.

China already has one of the highest debt-to-GDP ratios in the world (second only to Japan's), and it is rising at perhaps the fastest rate in history. Like Japan's, in other words, China's manufacturing success depends ultimately on an unsustainable increase in the country's debt burden.

But while this cannot go on forever, Busby is right to say that EV producers in the US (and Europe) should nonetheless be very concerned. As the history of the US chemical industries demonstrates, a county's productive capacity in a particular sector can be undermined in a very short period, but it is extremely difficult to rebuild.
https://www.thewirechina.com/2026/07/12/americas-ev-reckoning/

items.title: An elevator pitch on how the industrial gases industry works! You separate air into gases, or you buy rare gases and repackage them. You sell either f...
items.description_html:
An elevator pitch on how the industrial gases industry works!

You separate air into gases, or you buy rare gases and repackage them. You sell either from a plant you own (merchant) or from a plant you build inside a customer's factory (on-site).

On-site is a 15-20 year annuity at thin margins. A merchant is a spot market where the same molecule earns very different money depending on the gas, the distance, and who else is selling nearby.

The whole game is capital, location, and the ability to squeeze extra high-margin gas (argon, liquid) out of a plant you've already built.

But let’s get into interesting details about that -

What does the industry actually sell?

Strip away the chemistry and this is an air-separation business. A cryogenic plant is an Air Separation Unit, or ASU that chills atmospheric air until its components liquefy at their different boiling points, and pipes off oxygen, nitrogen and argon at high purity. Everyone in the industry runs broadly the same technology, so purity differences are marginal (roughly plus-or-minus 5%). The molecule is a commodity. Almost everything interesting happens around the molecule in how it's contracted, delivered, and priced.

The demand base has historically been the heavy stuff: steel and metals, autos, pharma, healthcare, and a thin slice of chemicals and F&B. What's changing the growth story is the sunrise demand from solar and semiconductors, which pull large volumes of nitrogen and, in semis, demand purity that cheaper suppliers struggle to hit. That single shift is why every player in the country is pouring capital into new capacity at the same time.

The top four global MNCs Linde, INOX Air Products, Air Water India and Air Liquide India control roughly 80% of the market.

Below them sit regional, owner-driven players (Ellenbarrie, Vayu, Shree Ram Oxygas and similar) who have been around 10–15 years and whose space widened after COVID, when oxygen subsidies pulled local capital in.

Now let’s understand the 2 engines in this industry separately > on-site vs merchant

Everything downstream flows from one fork in the road: do you build the plant inside the customer's factory, or near a cluster of customers and truck the gas out? These are two genuinely different businesses with different economics, different customers, and different personalities.

> On-site - An anchor customer, usually a large steel plant, issues an RFQ saying, in effect, we need ~1,100–1,800 tonnes of gas a day flowing into our furnace. A supplier builds and operates an ASU on their premises under a Build-Own-Operate (BOO) contract running 15–20 years. The revenue is fixed and stable, so it's underwritten on a modest return — players will accept an operating margin of roughly 10–12%, sometimes pricing the whole thing to a ~10–15% IRR. It is deliberately boring money.

Why fight so hard for boring money? Because it's stable, long-dated and defensive with a 15–20 year foothold that also keeps a rival out of that customer. Contracts signed before roughly 2016–18 carried fatter margins and some still run (a few end around 2035), but the new-build market is now openly competitive on price.

> Merchant business - Here you own the plant, liquefy the output, and sell it as liquid, bulk or cylinders to whoever's within reach. Margins are structurally better but volatile: liquid/bulk runs a ~20–25% bottom line, while cylinder and specialty can reach ~40%. The catch is geography. Oxygen and nitrogen are cheap and heavy, so you can't economically truck them much beyond ~200–300 km before distribution cost (typically 10–12% of revenue) eats you alive. You plant an ASU, draw a 200–300 km radius, and fight for that circle.

The reason branded MNCs earn ~40–45% at the top while a strong regional player earns ~35% isn't a secret formula rather it's scale and mix. A Linde or INOX moves 4,000–5,000 TPD daily; a regional player moves 250–300 TPD. At a small scale, tender-driven medical demand keeps margins decent. But to scale from 300 TPD to 3,000–4,000 TPD you must chase the lower-margin sectors that make up the bulk of the market and that's exactly where pricing and margin pressure begin.

The value chain, end to end

Follow a molecule of oxygen from the atmosphere to the customer and you can see where value is captured and where it leaks.

Stage 1 - Build the plant (the capital gate)
This is a capital-intensive, power-intensive industry, and the entry cost is the first moat. A modest 200–250 TPD on-site plant needs at least INR 300–400 crore of initial capex. Core components are compressors, cold boxes that have to be imported; India makes none of them. Only fabrication and storage tanks (INOX, Cryolor, VRV) are domestic. Build time runs 16–24 months per ASU

Stage 2 - Choose your engineering partner (the quiet moat)
Who actually builds the plant is becoming the real differentiator. The MNCs carry in-house EPC muscle - Linde has Linde Engineering, INOX leans on Air Products' US technology which lets them build large, reliable ASUs and win the technically demanding jobs.
Regional players without that engineering wing increasingly tie up with Chinese OEMs on a sale-of-equipment basis: the Chinese firm builds and exits, and the local player does the O&M. It's cheaper (a 200 TPD plant runs ~INR 100–120 crore via a Chinese vendor vs ~INR 150–200 crore via an MNC), but blue-chip steelmakers (Tata, JSW, SAIL, AM/NS) often won't allow a Chinese machine where they're underwriting 15–20 years of reliability, power efficiency and capital backup, and they pay up for it.

The cost-vs-capability gap
> MNC plant, 200 TPD: ~INR 150–200 crore — technology, reliability, blue-chip acceptance.
> Chinese plant, 200 TPD: ~INR 100–120 crore — cost-optimised, favoured by smaller / secondary steel and rolling mills.
> No Indian regulation restricts Chinese ASUs, so the split is driven purely by which customer is buying.

Stage 3 - Extract the by-products (where margin hides)
A basic ASU makes oxygen and nitrogen. To pull argon you must bolt on a separate argon-recovery column and here's the quirk: that column costs roughly the same whether the plant is small or large, but a small 200 TPD ASU yields only ~5–6 TPD of argon while a big ASU yields 50–60 TPD. The capital-to-recovery maths favours scale heavily, yet argon's economics are so attractive that even smaller players are now installing the column anyway.

Stage 4 - Distribute (the tyranny of distance)
Oxygen and nitrogen are regional products which are heavy, cheap, and uneconomic beyond ~300 km. Argon is a national product valuable enough to ship across the country, though distribution then runs ~17–18% of revenue rather than 10–12%. This distinction quietly shapes the entire competitive map: oxygen/nitrogen competition is local and radius-bound, while argon competition is national and cyclical.

How to judge any player in this industry?
Pulling the mechanics together, a handful of questions separate a structurally strong operator from a fragile one regardless of the name on the door.

> Capacity trajectory: Can they actually double liquid capacity? You can't sell a molecule if you can't make expansion of 200 TPD takes 1.5 - 2 years, not six months.

> Mix quality: How much on-site annuity vs volatile merchant? How much argon and by-product upside are they extracting per plant?

> Engineering independence: In-house EPC, or dependent on Chinese OEMs who build and exit? This gates which customers they can win.

> Geographic competition: Are their plants in pockets with few rivals inside the 300 km radius, or in contested western/central India?

> Blue-chip acceptance: Can they clear the reliability/purity bar for Tata, JSW, AM/NS, and semiconductors or are they confined to secondary, cost-driven demand?

> Cycle timing: Are they adding capacity into the current glut, and can their balance sheet and geography absorb 2–3 years of merchant price erosion before demand catches up?

Dislaimer - not a recommendation to buy/sell

items.title: Creative Newtech has done an interesting acquisition, transforming from a distributor to a manufacturer of an integrated surveillance technology platf...
items.description_html:
Creative Newtech has done an interesting acquisition, transforming from a distributor to a manufacturer of an integrated surveillance technology platform.

Creative Newtech has approved the acquisition of 100% of Infinova (India) for up to US$4 million, marking a strategic shift beyond distribution into surveillance technology manufacturing.

The acquisition brings Infinova's India operations, brand rights, technical expertise, experienced workforce, and local assembly & manufacturing facility, providing Creative Newtech with immediate execution capabilities.

More importantly, the deal lays the foundation for a 'Make in India' surveillance platform, enabling the company to build proprietary products, strengthen after-sales support, and reduce dependence on third-party distribution.

The transaction also accelerates Creative's transition from a distribution-led business to an integrated, technology-led surveillance solutions provider, giving it greater control over manufacturing, product ownership, and margins.

The company holds all the relevant licenses from Gem, BIS, and STQC, enabling it to access the government portal. STQC shifts 22% market share from Chinese to Indian brands

This is more than an acquisition—it is a business model transformation, positioning Creative Newtech to move up the value chain from distributing surveillance products to designing, assembling, and manufacturing them under a 'Make in India' strategy.

Creative NewTech trades at 18 PE vs Prizor Vtech 42PE vs Aditya Infotech 110PE

A unique re-rating candidate driven by undervaluation, business transformation, and sector tailwinds

items.title: The Real Story Isn't Performance. It's Who Gets Paid Again. MGNREGA becomes VB G RAM G with a bigger ₹92,550cr pot, but the standout is West Bengal, ...
items.description_html:
The Real Story Isn't Performance. It's Who Gets Paid Again.

MGNREGA becomes VB G RAM G with a bigger ₹92,550cr pot, but the standout is West Bengal, from ₹0 under a 2022 freeze to a 9.19% share. Formula first, performance later. (Business Standard)

Our view:

This is a domestic fiscal story with real distributional teeth. MGNREGA becomes VB G RAM G with a bigger corpus, ₹92,550cr interim against ₹76,031cr released last year, but the money now flows through the 16th FC formula instead of raw demand, which quietly reweights the map. The tell is West Bengal, frozen out since 2022 and shown at ₹0 under MGNREGA, reappearing with a 9.19% share, while demand-heavy states like Tamil Nadu (10.13% of FY26 releases) sit well above their formula weight. The performance layer from FY28 is the lever the Centre keeps. Breaks if the "demand-driven" promise holds and formula shares turn out to be floors, not caps, in which case the redistribution is smaller than the table implies.

items.title: The AI Trade's Quietest Chokepoint Isn't a Chip ABF substrate TAM is set to compound at 33% through 2028, reaching roughly $18bn, with ROE heading to ...
items.description_html:
The AI Trade's Quietest Chokepoint Isn't a Chip

ABF substrate TAM is set to compound at 33% through 2028, reaching roughly $18bn, with ROE heading to a fresh record near 32%. The GPU can't ship without this film. (Goldman Sachs)

Our View

India localised the assembly floor but left the substrate layer, the exact thing this upcycle is about, almost entirely to Japan and Taiwan. A 33% CAGR in AI substrates makes that gap expensive to keep ignoring. The honest catch is that organic ABF is a twenty-year craft India cannot copy overnight, so the realistic entry is one of two doors: localise substrate as a mandated supplier feeding domestic OSAT, or leapfrog into glass-core substrate, where the whole industry is still early and the head start is measured in quarters, not decades. Breaks if ISM keeps funding assembly while leaving substrate to imports, in which case India stays a packaging floor with the valuable film sourced abroad.

items.title: India's Boring Balance Sheet Is the Feature, Not the Bug India-first flip. India's sub-50% credit ratio looks unambitious next to Vietnam, until you r...
items.description_html:
India's Boring Balance Sheet Is the Feature, Not the Bug

India-first flip. India's sub-50% credit ratio looks unambitious next to Vietnam, until you remember that under-leverage is what gives you room to run when the cycle turns.

Our take :

The eye goes to Vietnam, but the quiet signal is India's own restraint. India's private credit to GDP sits near the bottom of this pack, well under half Vietnam's level, and that gap is usually read as a weakness. Flip it. Low leverage is dry powder. It means India's credit cycle still has room to expand for years before it looks anything like Vietnam's, which supports a longer runway for lenders and credit-sensitive demand at home.

items.title: RT Nimesh Shah: Nomura on L&T Fin Buy, TP Rs 370 1QFY27 net profit beat our estimate by 8%; asset-quality trends encouraging Encouraging asset-quality...
items.description_html:
RT Nimesh Shah
Nomura on L&T Fin
Buy, TP Rs 370
1QFY27 net profit beat our estimate by 8%; asset-quality trends encouraging
Encouraging asset-quality trends, especially 2W segment with AI engines in place
Risks – Monsoon and upcoming insurance distribution reform
Lift FY27-29F EPS estimates by 2-10%

JPM on L&T Fin
Neutral, TP Rs 320
L&T Finance reported a strong 1QFY27 with PPoP at Rs 20.2bn (+35% y/y) & PAT at Rs 9.02bn (+29% y/y, +12% q/q) both coming 4% ahead of JPMe.
While NII at Rs 26.8bn (+31% y/y, +5% q/q) came 1% lower vs JPMe on lower NIMs, adverse impact was more than offset by
(i) higher fee income which rose by 22.7% y/y to Rs 6bn (+12% vs JPMe),
(ii) lower opex (-2.6% vs JPMe). NIM (on AUM) declined 10bps q/q (-9bps vs JPMe) which is likely attributable to the LTF maintaining a higher liquidity buffer during the quarter on account of geopolitical uncertainty
On Asset Quality, while GS2+GS3 declined by 18bps q/q to 4.06% (-18bps q/q, -16bps vs JPMe), loan losses at Rs 8bn came 2% above JPMe with credit costs coming 5bps higher vs JPMe at 2.55% (-6bps q/q) primarily driven by a 111/175bps q/q rise in Stage-2/Stage-3 PCR.
Net slippages (change in GS-3+write-offs) rose by 17bps q/q to 2.14%
While there is an element of seasonality, AQ trends will be closely watched especially given the concerns around monsoons
Reported RoA of 2.48% rose 8bps q/q and came 13bps ahead of JPMe.
AUM growth of 27% y/y was retail-led with retail disbursements at Rs 238.5bn (+36% y/y, -1% q/q).
LTF is up 23% in last 1M (vs NIFTY +2.5%), with valuations expanding to 2.3x FY28E PB for 14% RoE.
This likely limits the scope for further re-rating
@CNBCTV18Live @_RituSingh

items.title: Japan Runs Out of People, One Industry at a Time Autos must triple their foreign workforce share to 27% by 2040 just to hold output flat. Farm product...
items.description_html:
Japan Runs Out of People, One Industry at a Time

Autos must triple their foreign workforce share to 27% by 2040 just to hold output flat. Farm production value halves by 2050. A third of domestic freight goes unshipped by 2030. This is not one labour shortage. It is an economy running down its headcount sector by sector.

The shortage that automation can't paper over. Japan already runs more than three times the global robot density and it has not solved the labour gap, because the missing workers are drivers, farmers and carers, not assembly-line hands. The read is that the demographic squeeze is a services and blue-collar problem, and capex on factory robots is the wrong tool for most of it.

items.title: US households are sitting on a massive cash pile despite record equity exposure: Household equity holdings are up to a record ~$72 trillion. This is $...
items.description_html:
US households are sitting on a massive cash pile despite record equity exposure:

Household equity holdings are up to a record ~$72 trillion.

This is $18 trillion, or +33%, above its long-term trend from 2009 to 2019.

Household stock ownership has more than doubled since 2020.

Meanwhile, cash and cash equivalent holdings are up to $20 trillion, an all-time high.

This exceeds the long-term trend by $5 trillion, or +33%.

By comparison, households also own a record $11 trillion in debt securities such as bonds, $2.2 trillion, or +25%, above trend.

American households have never had a bigger cash buffer.

items.title: RT The Kobeissi Letter: Shocking stat of the day: Nvidia, $NVDA, Micron, $MU, Broadcom, $AVGO, and Applied Materials, $AMAT, are now expected to gener...
items.description_html:
RT The Kobeissi Letter
Shocking stat of the day:

Nvidia, $NVDA, Micron, $MU, Broadcom, $AVGO, and Applied Materials, $AMAT, are now expected to generate a record $430 billion in combined free cash flow (FCF) over the next 12 months.

That would be more than TRIPLE the FCF they generated just 2 years ago.

At the same time, the combined FCF of Amazon, $AMZN, Alphabet, $GOOGL, Meta, $META, Microsoft, $MSFT, and Oracle, $ORCL, is projected to turn negative for the first time on record.

That would mark a massive reversal from the +$260 billion peak reported by these companies in 2024.

This comes as AI-related CapEx by these 5 companies is estimated to surge to ~$1.8 trillion in 2026 and 2027 combined.

Chipmakers are becoming cash machines, while AI giants are burning record amounts of capital.

items.title: My average cost in Solara is 500-525, and I never added below 400 or above 820. My mistake was going in too early and underestimating the risk from Ib...
items.description_html:
My average cost in Solara is 500-525, and I never added below 400 or above 820. My mistake was going in too early and underestimating the risk from Ibuprofen. Arun Kumar had already been quite clear that competition in Ibuprofen was intensifying, and in early 2025 he publicly praised the challengers, IOLCP and SMS Pharma, on an earnings call. I let my own bias and incomplete reading of the situation override the warning signs. With Viyash Scientific, I was probably just fortunate to have accumulated at much lower levels.
Mistakes are part of building a broader portfolio, but they should never be allowed to become ruinous or terminal. There is no shame in openly acknowledging that my error was timing, specifically being too early in Solara.

Disc: none of this is an open invitation or recommendation

items.title: RT Archaeological Survey of India: The Dhandi Temple ruins are located at Gohpur in Biswanath, Assam. The temple was commissioned in 1705 CE by Ahom K...
items.description_html:
RT Archaeological Survey of India
The Dhandi Temple ruins are located at Gohpur in Biswanath, Assam. The temple was commissioned in 1705 CE by Ahom King Rudra Singha. It featured a distinctive octagonal plan on a raised platform and was built using traditional brickwork in lime mortar.

Although the historic structure collapsed during the 1897 Assam earthquake, its remains continue to serve as an important protected archaeological site, preserving the region's rich cultural and structural heritage.

items.title: Companies that consistently gain market share usually deserve a premium Because market share gain is not just about growth It reflects stronger execut...
items.description_html:
Companies that consistently gain market share usually deserve a premium

Because market share gain is not just about growth

It reflects stronger execution & the ability to scale better than peers

When a company keeps gaining share in a competitive industry, it means something is working structurally in its favour

Markets usually reward such companies with higher valuations because they are not just growing with the industry, they are outperforming the industry

This is why tracking market share trends is extremely important

Sustainable market share gain = valuation re-rating

items.title: Every year, PSU banks were consistently losing credit market share to private banks From FY16 to FY24, PSU banks’ share declined from 68.1% to 51.8% ...
items.description_html:
Every year, PSU banks were consistently losing credit market share to private banks

From FY16 to FY24, PSU banks’ share declined from 68.1% to 51.8%

But the trend has changed over the last 2 years

PSU banks have now gained credit market share for 2 consecutive years

Their share improved from 51.8% in FY24 to 52.3% in FY25 & further to 52.9% in FY26

Better asset quality, stronger capital position, improved profitability & retail loan growth are helping PSU banks regain market share

items.title: I spoke to a friend whos working on AI in a TOP US company and he had these big warnings 1. He says right now most top orgs are pushing demos and test...
items.description_html:
I spoke to a friend whos working on AI in a TOP US company and he had these big warnings

1.  He says right now most top orgs are pushing demos and tests all AI generated in the hope that something clicks
2. Lot of Fomo in big companies, if Company A did a new launch, goalpost internally shifts to build that using Claude Code or Codex
3. They acknowledge that the Tokens they are using has no significant revenue output
4. Most junior devs have stopped using their brains and prompt claude for everything
5. Lot of Technical Debt being built in todays platform, new code built on top of current AI generated code. Predicts in 1-2years this will break
6. Predicts that the day top management realizes Tokens and compute is not getting enough revenue, then big layoffs and budget cuts might happen

Crazy scary times - People invested in AI trade - Dont risk too much :)

items.description_html:
RT goldenlabubuwatch
The two listed Chinese AI lab companies http://Z.ai and Minimax already burned through 80-90% of the funds raised for compute at their IPOs in January 2026 (combined usd 1.2bn), six months ahead of schedule.

So they raided the HK market late this week (literally when lock up expired) for a combined usd 6bn raise, which is 5x what they raised at IPO.

See Timmy, this is why we have public markets. One annoying year preparing and selling an IPO, then one afternoon and a few phone calls to raise cash.

Cloud providers will receive 80% of these funds (Alibaba Cloud thankyou very much).

These in in turn will pass them on to compute equipment providers. How much of that goes to Huawei vs other local GPU guys vs Nvidia vs CPUs vs memory vs all the other equipment is anyone's guess (I'm sure someone will make up something that sounds thoughtful).

items.title: RT U.S. Central Command: At 7:15 p.m. ET today, U.S. Central Command forces began launching the third round of strikes this week against Iran after Is...
items.description_html:
RT U.S. Central Command
At 7:15 p.m. ET today, U.S. Central Command forces began launching the third round of strikes this week against Iran after Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps forces blatantly attacked M/V GFS Galaxy, a Cyprus-flagged container ship transiting the Strait of Hormuz. A civilian crew member is missing and the vessel is unable to continue the journey due to an onboard fire and significant engineroom damage.

Iran was provided yet another opportunity to demonstrate adherence to the Memorandum of Understanding after being held accountable for earlier attacks on commercial vessels but has again failed.

In response, the United States is imposing a heavy cost by continuing to degrade Iran’s ability to attack civilian mariners and commercial ships freely transiting the strait. The strikes are being carried out at the direction of the Commander in Chief.

items.description_html:
RT Surenddra verma
Great Punjabi Lala Jagat Narain.
He spent years in prison for opposing both British rule and the Emergency. He built Punjab Kesari into one of Punjab's most widely read newspapers and refused to bow before terror.

He stood up to Bhindranwale despite repeated threats. He was assassinated for his courage. The sacrifices did not end there-his son, Ramesh Chander, was also killed, along with 52 members of the Punjab Kesari family.

The media group lost:
• 2 Editors
• 7 Reporters
• 20 Distribution Agents
• 23 Hawkers
• 2 Workers

Their only "crime" was refusing to surrender to Khalistani intimidation and continuing to print the truth.

Punjab Kesari also raised funds and supported the families of Hindu and Sikh civilians murdered by Khalistani terrorists when the state had largely failed them.

When fear forced distributors to stop carrying the newspaper, KPS Gill ensured copies were distributed through police stations so the voice of truth would not be silenced.

Lala Jagat Narain, the Punjab Kesari family, and KPS Gill remind us that Punjab was saved not only by brave security personnel but also by fearless journalists and ordinary citizens who refused to bow before terror.

Their sacrifice must never be forgotten. 🇮🇳

items.title: RT Vikas Saraswat: Why is it so difficult to be a KPS Gill in the system? It is difficult because the overwhelming number of them, be it a bureaucrat,...
items.description_html:
RT Vikas Saraswat
Why is it so difficult to be a KPS Gill in the system? It is difficult because the overwhelming number of them, be it a bureaucrat, a politician, a Cop or even a Judge fear the calumny and ad hominem that might come their way for doing their duty and upholding the integrity of the nation. Tough circumstances demand tough response. When terrorists have breached all bounds of civility and there is a large number cheering, sympathising and sheltering them, the state response cannot remain bound to probity and protocols. When terrorists have complete control over everything from clothing to spoken language, State cannot keep waiting for acts of terror to happen first before getting up and react. It will gear towards preventive action and discretion will be exercised. There will always be a possibility of excesses which in hindsight will constitute human right violations. It requires someone with extraordinary courage and commitment to stand up to the occasion and go for the charge. Such a person knows that he can be hounded by bleeding heart liberals once the embers have cooled. Such a person knows that there will be enough men and women who will curse and abuse him for shattering their secessionist and chauvinist dreams. Such a person knows there will be an Army of virtue signallers who, without knowing the circumstances, would villainise him just to appear cool and righteous. Added to that there is always a threat to life for not only the person but also his kith and kin. And yet this man risks all of it because for him nation and long term peace comes first. Unlike a fence sitting, risk averse, tightrope walker in the system who allows the tactical window to close and the crisis to spiral completely out of control, the call of duty tells this hero that “he who hesitates is lost”. KPS Gill was one such hero. While he was cleaning the mess he would have already anticipated the abuses and insults which would come his way one day. Gill risked ignominy for the sake of righteousness. That makes him an even bigger hero, a giant among pygmies and a worthy son of Mother India.

items.title: RT Mickey from Arcadia: The 10 craziest parts of the ChatGPT lawsuit. Apple says: 1.) A former Apple engineer named Chang Liu quit Apple in January 20...
items.description_html:
RT Mickey from Arcadia
The 10 craziest parts of the ChatGPT lawsuit.

Apple says:

1.) A former Apple engineer named Chang Liu quit Apple in January 2026 to join OpenAI, secretly kept his Apple-issued laptop instead of returning it, then discovered a bug that let him log back into Apple's private servers from his new desk at OpenAI.

He didn't report the bug. He didn't log off. He texted a friend still working at Apple: "LOL, I found out I can access the [server], so funny." Then he spent weeks downloading over 1,000 pages of Apple's confidential engineering files while actively building competing hardware for OpenAI.

2.) Apple is suing OpenAI's Chief Hardware Officer a man named Tang Yew Tan who spent 24 years at Apple as VP of Product Design for iPhone and Apple Watch before leaving to co-found OpenAI's hardware division.

Apple alleges that when Tan interviewed Apple employees for jobs at OpenAI, he told them to physically bring Apple hardware to the interview. Batteries. Circuit boards. Logic boards. Prototype parts. Actual physical components taken from Apple's facilities.

One candidate was so caught off guard he said he "didn't even know we could take those from the office."

3.) Apple's lawsuit reveals that OpenAI was circulating a confidential internal Apple document, one marked "Need to Know," intended only for Apple's own managers, among new OpenAI hires.

The document details Apple's security procedures for when an employee resigns.

OpenAI was sharing it with recruits before they even told Apple they were leaving, so they knew exactly what checks to expect and how to avoid them. OpenAI employees referred to it as "a checklist that Tang put together" even though it was clearly an Apple internal document.

4.) Apple's investigation found that OpenAI was actively coaching Apple employees on how to handle their exit from Apple.

The advice included: don't sign anything at your exit interview. And if Apple asks you to sign something, contact OpenAI immediately. OpenAI was running legal interference on Apple's own offboarding process in real time, from the inside.

5.) Tang Tan, the former Apple VP who is now OpenAI's Chief Hardware Officer, was reportedly warning Apple employees he was recruiting not to tell Apple they had accepted jobs at OpenAI.

The strategy: stay at Apple as long as possible, keep your badge and system access active, and keep gathering information.

Apple says it found a clear pattern of departing employees skipping exit interviews, ignoring security outreach, and taking steps to evade the procedures designed to protect Apple's confidential data.

6.) OpenAI's interview process for hardware candidates, many of them current Apple employees, required them to prepare a "Technical Deep Dive" presentation with slides about their work.

The instructions asked candidates to disclose which vendors Apple uses, how Apple selects hardware components, what software tools Apple uses for system integration, and how Apple manages its supplier relationships.

7.) Apple's lawsuit alleges that OpenAI interviewers, former Apple insiders who knew the company's internal language, were using Apple's secret project code names during job interviews to ask candidates about unreleased Apple products.

Apple documented at least one case where a candidate began screenshotting and downloading confidential Apple files in the hours before his OpenAI interview.

During the interview, Tang Tan asked him about the exact same project. Apple says this has become an established pattern.

8.) Apple alleges that OpenAI, through its hardware subsidiary io Products, approached one of Apple's trusted industrial design partners and had them perform Apple's proprietary metal-finishing technique the multi-step process Apple developed over years to produce the distinctive look and feel of its products.

OpenAI told the partner that Apple had given permission. Apple had given no such permission. The partner was bound by exclusivity agreements with Apple that explicitly prohibited doing this work for anyone else.

9.) The engineer who broke back into Apple's servers, Chang Liu wasn't acting alone. Apple's lawsuit alleges he was simultaneously coaching a current Apple employee named Alyssa Peng on how to copy files from Apple workstations without triggering the security team, directing her to specific confidential project folders, and using Apple's stolen data to help her prepare for her own OpenAI job interview.

To keep all of this hidden, Liu told Peng to stop communicating over Apple devices and switch to a separate private messaging app called LINE.

10.) Apple's lawsuit frames this not as the actions of a few rogue employees, but as a deliberate institutional strategy. OpenAI now employs over 400 former Apple engineers and executives.

Apple alleges that OpenAI built its entire hardware recruiting pipeline around extracting Apple's proprietary knowledge from manufacturing processes and supplier relationships to unreleased product designs and that as a result, OpenAI's nascent hardware business is, in Apple's words, "rotten to its core by its illegal reliance on misappropriated trade secrets."

items.title: RT Film Updates: Christopher Nolan on AI: “I’ve never seen a more rapid wholesale dismissal of a supposedly foundational jump in technology in my li...
items.description_html:
RT Film Updates
Christopher Nolan on AI:

“I’ve never seen a more rapid wholesale dismissal of a supposedly foundational jump in technology in my lifetime. So much energy has been expended on bringing in AI, but if you look at that generation’s reaction, they’re utterly rejecting it. [My children’s] judgment of AI slop has been immediate and harsh. They see it for what it is very quickly – and it’s much easier for them to identify it, because it grew out of an online world they know really well. And while that doesn’t mean that every aspect of the technology is useless or meaningless, in film-making it’s hitting at exactly the wrong time. After years of driving towards heavily virtual environments, we’re seeing a renewed interest in more tactile, more real forms of storytelling.”

🔗 https://www.telegraph.co.uk/films/2026/07/10/christopher-nolan-interview-odyssey-backlash/

items.title: RT Parimal: Next in who after the Ramanujan series? At a tender age of 16, a brilliant but completely unguided boy from Tamil Nadu joined the National...
items.description_html:
RT Parimal
Next in who after the Ramanujan series?

At a tender age of 16, a brilliant but completely unguided boy from Tamil Nadu joined the National Defence Academy & was commissioned into the Indian Navy's electrical branch . He did not have the luxury of a quiet university setting; he was trained in practical skills to maintain weapons systems on warships.

But Arogyaswami Paulraj possessed an insatiable, self-taught obsession with the advanced mathematics of signal processing, control theory & information theory. He studied advanced matrices & random variables by lamplight on naval ships.

By the late 1970s, India faced a serious strategic challenge. After the 1971 Indo-Pak War exposed weaknesses in imported sonars, the Navy needed an advanced anti-submarine warfare system but was blocked by international export restrictions. The Navy turned to Paulraj, then a rising officer with a PhD from IIT Delhi (earned while still in service). He was tasked with leading a major indigenous project to develop a world-class hull-mounted panoramic sonar from scratch.

Operating under intense resource scarcity, Paulraj’s mathematical genius took over. He designed complex signal-processing algos that could filter the chaotic, deafening acoustic noise of the ocean to pinpoint enemy submarines. The resulting system, APSOH (Advanced Panoramic Sonar Hull), inducted in 1983, completely stunned global military observers. It did not just work, it outperformed contemporary Western systems.

After setting up major defense labs in India, Paulraj retired from active naval service & arrived at Stanford University in 1991 as a research associate. This is where the story shifts from military history to modern legend. While working on signal separation experiments for airborne military reconnaissance, Paulraj noticed a strange, fleeting physical phenomenon.

When a radio signal is transmitted in a crowded area (like a city with buildings), it bounces off walls & scatters into 1000s of chaotic, distorted paths. Engineers treated this scattering as a nightmare, multipath interference that corrupted data.

Paulraj had a paradigm-shifting realization rooted in multi-variable calculus & spatial matrices: What if the scattering was not a bug, but a feature?

He realized that if we used multiple antennas at the transmitter & multiple antennas at the receiver, we could use advanced matrix mathematics to isolate those scattered paths & stream parallel, independent channels of data over the exact same frequency, at the exact same time.

He called it MIMO (Multiple Input, Multiple Output).

When he 1st proposed it, the academic world mocked him. Prominent profs & industry skeptics told him it violated the laws of physics & information theory. They claimed it was mathematically impossible to multiply data speeds w/o expanding bandwidth.

Paulraj did no back down. He built his own prototype, founded a startup & proved the mathematics in real-world silicon. He designed the microscopic architecture, the microchip algos that allowed small devices to execute these hyper-complex spatial matrix calculations in fractions of a microsecond.

If we look at the device we are using to read this right now, look at the top corners of our screen. We cannot see them, but embedded inside the frame of our phone are multiple microscopic antennas operating on Paulraj’s exact MIMO-OFDMA mathematics.

Every single modern 4G network, 5G network & high-speed Wi-Fi router on Earth is built entirely on the mathematical foundation invented by the self-taught Indian Navy officer who packed his bags for Stanford. He did not just solve a math problem; he built the invisible highway that carries nearly 100% of the world's mobile data traffic today.

items.title: The Underdog That Beat the Giants - In the 1980s, the lithography equipment industry was dominated by large American and Japanese companies. ASML was ...
items.description_html:
The Underdog That Beat the Giants -

In the 1980s, the lithography equipment industry was dominated by large American and Japanese companies.

ASML was just a small spin-off from Philips. The company started with limited resources. Its early machines were not particularly competitive.

Many customers preferred equipment from larger, established manufacturers. But ASML pursued a different strategy. Instead of simply improving existing designs, the company focused on aggressive innovation.

Engineers were encouraged to experiment with entirely new approaches to lithography. This culture created a steady stream of breakthroughs. Over time, competitors began falling behind. Some exited the market. Others struggled to keep up with the pace of technological change.

By the 2010s, the industry had consolidated dramatically. And in the field of EUV lithography, ASML had effectively become the only supplier.

Lesson:

Technological leadership can transform a small underdog into an industry gatekeeper!

items.title: RT Persistence Capital: We are pleasantly surprised by Q1FY27 business updates so far: Credit growth - accelerating and healthy Consumer (Retailers, a...
items.description_html:
RT Persistence Capital
We are pleasantly surprised by Q1FY27 business updates so far:

Credit growth - accelerating and healthy

Consumer (Retailers, auto OEMs, jewelers, FMCG, real estate) - YoY growth robust despite the overhang of war in Q1

USD$ services exports - YoY growth remains healthy despite deflationary impact of GenAI. Leasing data, office occupancy, square foot addition suggest that India is moving from back office to middle office. IT services revenue growth remains lackluster but GCCs (so far) are more than picking up the slack

Sunrise industries galore - Precision manufacturing, drones, specialty chemicals, CDMOs etc

Capital goods - Domestic business accelerating as India builds domestic DC footprint

Markets haven't moved for 22 months now in local currency terms. Valuations have time corrected. Growth trajectory remains healthy and more importantly, untethered to the AI capex build-out.

Hard to argue that India isn't interesting again for FIIs

Pharma Cheat Sheet

items.title: Learning of the day before going to sleep What are Solid State Transformers? 1. Solid-state transformers (SSTs) are advanced power devices that replac...
items.description_html:
Learning of the day before going to sleep

What are Solid State Transformers?

1. Solid-state transformers (SSTs) are advanced power devices that replace traditional, heavy copper-and-iron transformers with electronic circuitry. They use semiconductor switches and high-frequency internal components to change voltage.

2. Solid State Transformers are devices which enable real-time monitoring and control — something legacy transformers cannot do because it’s been all steel and copper.

3. Traditional transformers are passive, bulky blocks of metal that only adjust voltage. By contrast, SSTs act as the "smart router" of the electrical grid.

4.Incredible Shrinkage: SSTs operate at much higher frequencies than the normal grid (which operates at 50 or 60 Hz). The higher the frequency, the smaller the transformer core can be. This shrinks their size and weight by up to 50%, making them ideal for dense urban areas and modern data centers.

5. Two-Way Power Flow: Traditional transformers only allow power to flow in one direction. SSTs allow bidirectional power flow, meaning power can travel both to and from devices. This makes them essential for electric vehicles that need to feed power back to the grid and for homes with solar panels.

6. Smart Grid Control: SSTs use software and microchips to actively manage electricity. If the voltage drops, they adjust it instantly. They can filter out "dirty" power (like electrical noise or surges) to protect sensitive electronics, acting like a giant surge protector and regulator in one.

Must read article on Transformer shortage here

https://ig.ft.com/transformers/

items.title: The 10 Lakh crore Thermal Power Capex boom https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Funuk-of7t8&t=3018s How thermal Power is generated (1).Coal is used as fuel...
items.description_html:
The 10 Lakh crore Thermal Power Capex boom

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Funuk-of7t8&t=3018s

How thermal Power is generated

(1).Coal is used as fuel which is burned in a boiler to create intense heat

(2). That heat boils water into high-pressure, high-temperature steam

(3). The steam then rushes through and spins a turbine

(4). Converting the heat energy into spinning mechanical motion. This spinning turbine drives a generator.

(5) Which actually produces the electricity. The voltage is then stepped up by a transformer

(6) So the power can travel long distances, and finally it reaches the power grid.

(7) Grid delivers electricity to homes and industries.

items.title: RT menaka doshi: India’s AI Sovereignty Challenge: Winning Without Owning the Whole Stack The world’s fifth-largest economy has few strengths in the...
items.description_html:
RT menaka doshi
India’s AI Sovereignty Challenge: Winning Without Owning the Whole Stack

The world’s fifth-largest economy has few strengths in the AI ecosystem. It is broadly self-sufficient in energy but imports all advanced AI chips, accounts for only about 3% of global data-center capacity and has yet to produce a globally competitive frontier model.

At first glance, the conclusion appears obvious: India is behind.

That inference deserves more nuance, Srikanth Velamakanni (@srikanth) and Ganesh Ramakrishnan (@ganramkr) argued when they joined me on Bloomberg’s new Emerging podcast.

Watch here 👇
https://www.bloomberg.com/news/videos/2026-07-09/can-india-catch-up-in-the-ai-race-video?cmpid=india-edition&utm_campaign=india-edition&utm_medium=email&utm_source=newsletter&utm_term=260709&utm_content=6348

items.title: RT Reads with Ravi: Umberto Eco, who owned 50,000 books, had this to say about home libraries: “It is foolish to think that you have to read all the ...
items.description_html:
RT Reads with Ravi
Umberto Eco, who owned 50,000 books, had this to say about home libraries:

“It is foolish to think that you have to read all the books you buy, as it is foolish to criticize those who buy more books than they will ever be able to read. It would be like saying that you should use all the cutlery or glasses or screwdrivers or drill bits you bought before buying new ones.

“There are things in life that we need to always have plenty of supplies, even if we will only use a small portion.

“If, for example, we consider books as medicine, we understand that it is good to have many at home rather than a few: when you want to feel better, then you go to the ‘medicine closet’ and choose a book. Not a random one, but the right book for that moment. That’s why you should always have a nutrition choice!

“Those who buy only one book, read only that one and then get rid of it. They simply apply the consumer mentality to books, that is, they consider them a consumer product, a good. Those who love books know that a book is anything but a commodity.”

items.title: RT Sann: His name is Dinesh Thakur. He gave up a safe, high paying job and spent eight years of his life fighting one of the largest drug companies in...
items.description_html:
RT Sann
His name is Dinesh Thakur.

He gave up a safe, high paying job and spent eight years of his life fighting one of the largest drug companies in the country. He did it because he discovered that the medicines millions of people trusted were built on a lie.

He was a chemical engineer who had worked in America and come back to India for a senior role at Ranbaxy, then the biggest generic medicine maker in the country. It was a proud Indian company whose cheap medicines were sold across the world.

Not long after he joined, he was asked to look into the quality of the company's drugs. What he found frightened him.

The company was faking its test results. Medicines were being sold without being properly tested for whether they were safe, or whether they even worked. The data handed to regulators was simply invented.

These were not luxury goods. These were the tablets that sick people swallowed every day, trusting that they would heal them.

Thakur went to his own bosses with what he had found. Nothing changed. So he made a choice that cost him everything. He resigned, and he reported the company to the American drug regulators.

Then began eight long years. He handed over evidence, sat through investigation after investigation, and lived under the shadow of taking on a giant that had every reason to crush him.

In 2013, it ended. Ranbaxy admitted its guilt in an American court, pleaded guilty to seven serious crimes, and was made to pay one of the largest fines ever handed to a generic drug maker, five hundred million dollars.

Because one man refused to look away, the way the world checks the safety of its medicines was changed for good.

He had a comfortable life and a good salary. He gave it all up so that the medicine in a stranger's hand could be trusted.

items.title: Iran/US - Is it a blip or a breakdown ? @PramitWorld says that Iran felt betrayed because, despite the ceasefire, potential buyers were not buying the...
items.description_html:
Iran/US - Is it a blip or a breakdown ?

@PramitWorld says that Iran felt betrayed because, despite the ceasefire, potential buyers were not buying their oil due to a lack of "definite clearance" from US Treasury.

10 points from our chat today. Video below.

1. MoU was very vague to start with, a kind of a 'throw-together peace'

2. A clash or flare-up was inevitable at some point

3. Imposing sanctions is easy, undoing them is very difficult

4. Iranian oil were passing via SoH but with no buyers of the oil, due to US sanctions, Iran would have felt betrayed

5. Potential buyers of Iranian oil would have needed extraordinary level of assurance that they are not violating sanctions

6. Iran has pushed the envelope quite a bit w.r.t. SoH, emboldened by Guld states

7. I think the core position of U.S. remains the same - it isn't ready to use military force to reopen the SoH

8. I would imagine Iranian influence in the region would keep growing

9. Trump wants to get out of the war, keep US pump prices low, with a difficult midterms ahead - these reasons have not changed

10. Markets may take the long view that there is enough oil in the world, the Saudis want to pump a lot more & that no one wants to really fight.

#Nifty #BankNifty #Trump #IranWar‌ #Oil

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fnmcD_dmjig

RT Value Research
Can an algorithm really know when to cut equity exposure and when to add it back?

We ask Bhautik Ambani, CEO of AlphaGrep Mutual Fund, how the AlphaGrep Multi Asset Allocation Fund makes its allocation decisions, what its limitations are, and what investors should expect.

items.title: US President Donald Trump said his tentative ceasefire with Iran is over. “For me, I think it’s over,” he said on Wednesday in Ankara, sitting next...
items.description_html:
US President Donald Trump said his tentative ceasefire with Iran is over.
“For me, I think it’s over,” he said on Wednesday in Ankara, sitting next to NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte
at the military alliance’s annual summit. “As far as I’m concerned it’s just a waste of time.”
Trump’s remarks came shortly after the US launched a new wave of strikes against Iran and revoked a waived that allowed the sale of Iranian oil. The actions, taken in response to recent attacks on ships transiting the
Strait of Hormuz, brought fresh volatility to energy markets and tested an already fragile peace agreement between Washington and Tehran.
@business

items.title: RT Sumita Kale: Very important change that has been observed- time to go way beyond averages. Unless we appreciate what has changed, how will our urba...
items.description_html:
RT Sumita Kale
Very important change that has been observed- time to go way beyond averages. Unless we appreciate what has changed, how will our urban plans and models adapt?

"The altostratus clouds, which were once associated with the Indian monsoon, have become less common over the Indian territory, especially over Delhi-NCR. These clouds generally bring small quantities of rain over a prolonged period.
Such clouds have been replaced by cumulonimbus clouds, also known as thunderstorms, which are characterised by intense rain spells, lightning, hail, and strong winds."

"A senior official from the India Meteorological Department told ThePrint that climate change and extreme urbanisation are likely among the many factors behind this shifting monsoon trend and cloud formation patterns.

“Rainfall data confirms this trend. Over the last few years, we have been observing that rainfall that we used to get in a staggered period over the entire season is received in a few spells. The rest of the season remains dry,” the official said, adding that such a shift is neither suitable for urban centres nor conducive to agricultural activities."

Would be grateful for links to more research on this @RockSea @Hosalikar_KS @vineet_mausam Thanks!
@rapiduplift @sushmadate
https://theprint.in/environment/monsoon-indian-clouds-climate-change/2978458/

India wants to keep expanding oil refining capacities to hedge upon the global chokepoints

The current capex pipeline that is there -

Barmer is the one that got commissioned. The order book behind the 258 → 310 MMTPA path is what actually funds the ecosystem:

1) Panipat P-25 (IOCL): 15 → 25 MMTPA

7) Bina & Mumbai (BPCL): unit additions + Bina petchem

9) Andhra Refinery (BPCL): new grassroot
10) Ratnagiri West Coast (RRPCL — IOCL/BPCL/HPCL, ± Aramco/ADNOC): the 60 MMTPA long-shot mega-project still on the map
